quizQuestion: What type of physical quantity has magnitude only and no direction?
explanation: Scalars are fully described by a number and unit alone — no direction needed. Examples: distance (5 m), speed (30 m/s), mass (2 kg), temperature (300 K), and time (10 s). Confusing scalars with vectors is a common error on the AP exam.
visualDescription: A speedometer showing only a magnitude number with no arrow

quizQuestion: What type of physical quantity requires both magnitude AND direction to fully describe it?
explanation: Vectors need both a magnitude and a direction. Examples: displacement (5 m north), velocity (30 m/s east), acceleration (9.8 m/s² downward), and force (10 N at 30°). Vectors are often drawn as arrows, where length represents magnitude and orientation shows direction.
visualDescription: An arrow labeled with both a length (magnitude) and an angle showing direction

quizQuestion: Which quantity measures total path length traveled and is a scalar (not a vector)?
explanation: Distance measures the total length of the path traveled, regardless of direction. If you walk 3 m east then 4 m west, your distance is 7 m. It is always non-negative and has no directional component, making it a scalar.
visualDescription: A curvy path with total length labeled as distance

quizQuestion: What vector quantity equals the change in position from start to finish, regardless of path taken?
explanation: Displacement is defined as Δx = x_final − x_initial. It measures the straight-line change in position and includes direction. Walking 3 m east then 4 m west gives a displacement of 1 m west — not 7 m. Displacement can be zero even when distance is large (e.g., completing a lap on a track).
visualDescription: A straight arrow from start point to end point overlaid on a curved path

quizQuestion: What equation defines displacement (Δx) in one dimension?
explanation: Displacement Δx equals the final position x minus the initial position x₀. This simple subtraction gives you a signed result: positive values indicate motion in the positive direction, negative values indicate motion in the negative direction. It's the foundation of all kinematic equations.
visualDescription: A number line showing x₀ and x with an arrow labeled Δx between them

quizQuestion: What vector quantity equals displacement divided by the elapsed time (Δx/Δt)?
explanation: Average velocity = Δx/Δt. Because displacement is a vector, average velocity is also a vector — it has both magnitude and direction. It is NOT the same as average speed (which uses distance, a scalar). A car that returns to its starting point has zero average velocity for the trip, even though its average speed is non-zero.
visualDescription: Equation v_avg = Δx/Δt on a chalkboard with a direction arrow

quizQuestion: What is the defining equation for average velocity?
explanation: v_avg = Δx/Δt uses displacement (not distance) in the numerator. The SI unit is m/s. For an object moving at constant velocity, average velocity equals instantaneous velocity at every moment. For non-constant motion, v_avg gives only the overall rate of displacement change.
visualDescription: Fraction showing Δx over Δt with vector arrows indicating direction

quizQuestion: What equation defines average acceleration?
explanation: Average acceleration = Δv/Δt, where Δv = v_final − v_initial. Acceleration is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. An object can accelerate even while slowing down if acceleration opposes the direction of motion (deceleration). SI unit: m/s².
visualDescription: Equation a = Δv/Δt with velocity vectors at two different times

acceptableAlternatives: v final equals v initial plus a times t
quizQuestion: Which kinematic equation relates final velocity, initial velocity, acceleration, and time — with no displacement term?
explanation: v = v₀ + at applies when acceleration is constant. It is derived directly from the definition of acceleration: a = Δv/Δt → v − v₀ = at → v = v₀ + at. Use this when displacement is unknown or not needed. It is valid only for uniform (constant) acceleration.
visualDescription: Velocity-time graph with a straight line rising from v₀ to v over time t

acceptableAlternatives: displacement equals v-naught t plus one-half a t squared
quizQuestion: Which kinematic equation gives position as a function of time for constant acceleration (includes a ½at² term)?
explanation: x = x₀ + v₀t + ½at² describes position at any time t under constant acceleration. The ½at² term accounts for the changing velocity during the time interval. For free fall starting from rest (v₀ = 0), this reduces to x = x₀ + ½gt².
visualDescription: Parabolic trajectory of an object showing position growing quadratically with time

acceptableAlternatives: v squared equals v-naught squared plus 2a delta x
quizQuestion: Which kinematic equation relates velocity, acceleration, and displacement — with NO time variable?
explanation: v² = v₀² + 2aΔx is the time-independent kinematic equation. Use it when time is unknown and you need to find final velocity or displacement. For free fall: v² = v₀² + 2g(−h) when taking downward as negative. It is derived by eliminating t from the other two kinematic equations.
visualDescription: A ball dropped from height h with arrows showing v₀=0, v, and Δx

quizQuestion: What is the approximate magnitude of gravitational acceleration near Earth's surface used in AP Physics 1?
explanation: AP Physics 1 uses g ≈ 10 m/s² for calculations (the more precise value is 9.8 m/s²). This acceleration is directed downward toward Earth's center. All freely falling objects near Earth's surface — regardless of mass — experience this same downward acceleration (neglecting air resistance).
visualDescription: A falling ball with a downward arrow labeled g = 10 m/s²

acceptableAlternatives: slope of x-t graph equals velocity
quizQuestion: What physical quantity does the slope of a position-time (x-t) graph represent?
explanation: Slope = rise/run = Δx/Δt = velocity. On an x-t graph, a steep positive slope means fast motion in the positive direction; a negative slope means motion in the negative direction; a horizontal line means the object is at rest. A curved x-t graph has a slope (velocity) that changes over time.
visualDescription: Position-time graph with tangent line drawn at a point, labeled 'slope = velocity'

acceptableAlternatives: slope of v-t graph equals acceleration
quizQuestion: What physical quantity does the slope of a velocity-time (v-t) graph represent?
explanation: Slope = Δv/Δt = acceleration. A positive slope on a v-t graph means the object is speeding up in the positive direction; a negative slope could mean slowing down (if v is positive) or speeding up in the negative direction. A horizontal v-t line means zero acceleration (constant velocity).
visualDescription: Velocity-time graph with slope triangle labeled Δv over Δt = acceleration

quizQuestion: What physical quantity does the area under a velocity-time (v-t) graph represent?
explanation: Area under v-t graph = ∫v dt = displacement. For a constant velocity, the area is a rectangle (v × t = displacement). For constant acceleration, the area is a trapezoid or triangle. Crucially, area below the time axis counts as negative displacement (motion in the negative direction).
visualDescription: Shaded area under a v-t graph labeled 'area = displacement'

quizQuestion: What physical quantity does the area under an acceleration-time (a-t) graph represent?
explanation: Area under a-t graph = ∫a dt = Δv (change in velocity). This is the graphical interpretation of a_avg = Δv/Δt rearranged as Δv = a × Δt. It is NOT displacement; that would be the area under the v-t graph.
visualDescription: Shaded area under an a-t graph with label 'area = Δv'

quizQuestion: What type of reference frame moves at constant velocity (zero acceleration), so Newton's laws hold without modification?
explanation: An inertial reference frame is one that is not accelerating. In such frames, an object with no net force remains at constant velocity (Newton's First Law works directly). The laws of physics take the same form in all inertial frames — a key principle underlying classical mechanics. A rotating frame is non-inertial.
visualDescription: Two trains moving at constant velocity past each other, labeled as inertial frames

acceptableAlternatives: velocity relative to a reference frame
quizQuestion: What term describes the velocity of an object as measured from a particular reference frame, which can differ between observers?
explanation: Relative velocity depends on the observer's frame. A passenger on a train moving at 60 m/s east who walks 1 m/s toward the front has velocity 61 m/s east relative to the ground but only 1 m/s east relative to the train. To add relative velocities: v_A relative to C = v_A relative to B + v_B relative to C.
visualDescription: A person walking on a moving train with velocity arrows showing relative motion

quizQuestion: In ideal projectile motion (no air resistance), what is the horizontal component of acceleration?
explanation: With no air resistance, there is no horizontal force on a projectile, so horizontal acceleration = 0. Horizontal velocity remains constant throughout the flight. Only gravity acts, producing vertical acceleration of g ≈ 10 m/s² downward. This independence of horizontal and vertical motion is a core AP concept.
visualDescription: Projectile arc with horizontal velocity arrows of equal length and vertical arrows increasing downward

quizQuestion: In projectile motion, what is the vertical acceleration throughout the flight (near Earth's surface)?
explanation: Gravity causes a constant downward acceleration of g ≈ 10 m/s² throughout projectile flight. The vertical velocity changes by 10 m/s every second (increasing downward). At the peak of its arc, the projectile has zero vertical velocity but still has this full downward acceleration.
visualDescription: Projectile path with vertical velocity vectors growing downward at equal time intervals
distractors: g upward on way up, downward on way down

correctAnswer: Horizontal: A cos θ; Vertical: A sin θ
acceptableAlternatives: x-component = A cosθ, y-component = A sinθ
quizQuestion: For a vector A at angle θ above the horizontal, what are its x (horizontal) and y (vertical) components?
explanation: A_x = A cos θ (horizontal component) and A_y = A sin θ (vertical component), where θ is measured from the positive x-axis. To reconstruct the vector: magnitude A = √(A_x² + A_y²), direction θ = arctan(A_y/A_x). These decompositions allow 2D vector problems to be solved as two independent 1D problems.
visualDescription: Right triangle showing vector A, angle θ, and labeled components A_x and A_y
distractors: Horizontal: A sin θ; Vertical: A cos θ
Horizontal: A tan θ; Vertical: A cos θ
Horizontal: A/cos θ; Vertical: A/sin θ
Horizontal: A cos²θ; Vertical: A sin²θ
Horizontal: A sin²θ; Vertical: A cos²θ
Both equal A/√2 regardless of angle

acceptableAlternatives: square root of sum of squared components
quizQuestion: What equation gives the magnitude of a vector from its x and y components?
explanation: The Pythagorean theorem applies to vector components: |A| = √(A_x² + A_y²). For a projectile with v_x = 30 m/s and v_y = 40 m/s, the speed = √(900 + 1600) = √2500 = 50 m/s. The direction is then θ = arctan(A_y/A_x).
visualDescription: Right triangle with legs A_x and A_y and hypotenuse labeled |A|

quizQuestion: What scalar quantity equals the magnitude of velocity and is defined as distance traveled per unit time?
explanation: Speed = distance/time, and it is always non-negative. It is the scalar counterpart to velocity (a vector). Average speed = total distance / total time — not displacement/time. A car completing a lap at constant speed but returning to start has non-zero average speed but zero average velocity.
visualDescription: Speedometer gauge showing only a number with no directional indicator

quizQuestion: What term describes motion under gravity alone, with no air resistance, where all objects accelerate at the same rate regardless of mass?
explanation: In free fall, gravity is the only force acting. Near Earth's surface, all objects fall with the same downward acceleration g ≈ 10 m/s², regardless of mass. This was famously demonstrated by Galileo (allegedly at Pisa) and by the Apollo 15 hammer-feather experiment on the Moon. Air resistance breaks this equality.
visualDescription: A heavy ball and a feather falling side by side in a vacuum tube at the same rate

quizQuestion: What term describes the velocity of an object at one specific moment in time, equal to the slope of the x-t curve at that point?
explanation: Instantaneous velocity is the limit of Δx/Δt as Δt → 0. On a position-time graph, it equals the slope of the tangent line drawn at that specific point. Unlike average velocity (which covers an interval), instantaneous velocity captures motion at an exact instant — what a speedometer measures, with direction.
visualDescription: Position-time graph with a tangent line touching at one point, labeled as instantaneous velocity

correctAnswer: Opposite to the direction of motion (object is slowing down)
quizQuestion: If an object moves in the positive direction but its acceleration points in the negative direction, what is happening to its speed?
explanation: When acceleration and velocity point in opposite directions, the object slows down. Speed decreases because each moment the velocity gets a push opposite to its current direction. If acceleration continues, the object eventually stops, then moves in the negative direction. This is called deceleration or negative acceleration.
visualDescription: Velocity arrow pointing right with acceleration arrow pointing left, showing decreasing speed
distractors: The object moves at constant speed
The object has zero net force
The object is in free fall

correctAnswer: Range increases as launch angle approaches 45°
acceptableAlternatives: maximum range at 45 degrees launch angle
quizQuestion: For a projectile launched at the same initial speed, at what launch angle is horizontal range maximized (on level ground)?
explanation: For level ground and no air resistance, horizontal range R = v₀² sin(2θ)/g is maximized when sin(2θ) = 1, i.e., 2θ = 90°, so θ = 45°. Angles symmetric about 45° (e.g., 30° and 60°) yield identical ranges. The 45° result assumes equal launch and landing heights.
visualDescription: Three projectile arcs at 30°, 45°, 60° showing the 45° arc reaching the farthest horizontal distance
distractors: Range is independent of launch angle

acceptableAlternatives: location relative to a reference point
quizQuestion: What term describes the location of an object relative to a chosen reference point (origin), measured as a vector quantity?
explanation: Position is a vector quantity measured from a chosen reference point (origin) to the object's location. It requires a coordinate system and can be negative (left or below origin). Displacement = change in position = x_final − x_initial. Position itself (not displacement) tells you WHERE the object is, not how far it moved.
visualDescription: A number line with an origin and an object at position x, with a vector arrow from origin to object

quizQuestion: The three kinematic equations (v = v₀+at, x = x₀+v₀t+½at², v² = v₀²+2aΔx) are valid ONLY under what condition?
explanation: The kinematic equations apply ONLY when acceleration is constant (uniform). If acceleration changes over time, you cannot use these equations directly — you would need calculus (integration) or graphical methods. Free fall near Earth's surface qualifies because g is essentially constant. Circular motion does NOT qualify (centripetal acceleration changes direction).
visualDescription: A straight line on an a-t graph labeled 'constant acceleration' with the three kinematic equations listed

correctAnswer: Horizontal and vertical motions are independent of each other
acceptableAlternatives: independence of horizontal and vertical components
quizQuestion: What key principle allows projectile motion to be analyzed as two separate 1D problems?
explanation: In projectile motion, horizontal and vertical motions are completely independent. Horizontal: constant velocity (no air resistance). Vertical: constant downward acceleration g. They share only time as a common variable. This means a horizontally launched ball hits the ground at the SAME TIME as a ball dropped from the same height — both fall the same vertical distance in the same time.
visualDescription: Side-by-side comparison of dropped ball and horizontally launched ball reaching the ground simultaneously
distractors: Horizontal and vertical motions add together
Vertical motion depends on horizontal speed
Time of flight depends on horizontal velocity
Both components have equal acceleration g
The path shape depends on initial horizontal velocity
Horizontal speed decreases due to gravity
Air resistance affects horizontal motion more than vertical

acceptableAlternatives: average of initial and final velocities
quizQuestion: For constant acceleration, what useful equation gives average velocity as the mean of initial and final velocities?
explanation: When acceleration is constant, v_avg = (v₀ + v)/2. This makes sense because the velocity changes linearly, so the average is just the midpoint value. This is NOT valid for non-constant acceleration. It is often used alongside the kinematic equations to solve for unknowns — for example, combined with Δx = v_avg × t.
visualDescription: A v-t graph with initial velocity v₀ and final velocity v, and midpoint labeled as v_avg
